text:
Iran said Saturday that it’s closing the Strait of Hormuz, saying no vessels will be allowed to pass through until foreign interference ends, according state-run IRIB News.

The Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps also said it fired a warning shot on a cargo ship that tried to transit the strait on Saturday despite warnings, the outlet added.

Hedge Funds Focus on Refined Fuel Shortages

Investors remained very bearish about the outlook for petroleum prices before the latest upsurge in attacks on shipping in the Strait of Hormuz and the breakdown of the ceasefire between the United States and Iran.

But the overall bearish positioning concealed a growing divergence between extreme bearishness on crude and a more neutral or even bullish outlook for refined products, especially U.S. gasoline and European gasoil.

Crude markets remain oversupplied in the short term following a sharp rise in tanker transits through the Strait in recent weeks, though that could change quickly if the waterway is reclosed.

But inventories of refined fuels have been severely depleted since the start of the war between the United States and Iran.

The recent intensification of Ukraine’s long-range attacks on Russia’s oil refineries threatens to make fuel shortages worse.

Russia’s refineries mostly produce and export diesel and other middle distillates rather than gasoline but the loss of refining capacity will tighten markets for all fuels worldwide.

Refineries in North America, Europe and Asia forced to produce more diesel will be able to make less jet fuel and gasoline ...

This is a short extract from my newsletter emailed to clients earlier today. If you would like to read my newsletters in full, three times a week, you can subscribe via my website https://t.co/YjJTctVocX

NYT: Iran said on Sunday local time that it had opened fire on a vessel in the Strait of Hormuz and announced it was closing the critical waterway, rejecting a U.S. ultimatum and introducing new uncertainty to the course of the war

text:
Street Signal | Energy, Utilities and Mining Pulse: Responding to investor doubts, top ten investment portfolios unchanged in Q3 (Goldman Sachs)

Goldman Sachs released this report in response to investor questions about its Q3 energy portfolio. The report pointed out that despite the recent divergence of industry performance (refiners surged, oil and gas services/natural gas E&Ps fell), its core bullish logic remains unchanged.

The report shows through charts that oil refiners have performed best over the past 90 days, while natural gas E&Ps have been the worst.

Goldman Sachs believes that structural demands (such as electricity) and supply-side constraints (such as OPEC+ production cuts) brought about by energy transition are still the core factors that support energy prices and company profits in the medium and long term.

This shows that short-term fluctuations in the energy sector have not shaken Goldman Sachs' long-term confidence, and the current correction may be an opportunity to increase positions. One-sentence conclusion: The structural advantages of the energy sector (supply constraints + demand resilience) have not changed.

The current correction provides attractive entry opportunities and should focus on Goldman Sachs' top ten highlights stocks in Q3. Positive/Negative: Positive for refiners and integrated energy companies (such as XOM). Short-term positive for air-related natural gas E&P companies.

The market's pessimism about the energy sector has been partially released, but the sustainability of the sector rotation remains to be seen. Catalysts:

1) Crude oil and natural gas inventory data;

2) Global refinery operating rates;

3) OPEC+ production policy trends.

Read more: https://t.co/2iJL8mRbb4

Street Signal | European momentum stocks retreat, but fundamentals remain solid, energy sector bucks trend and rises (Goldman Sachs)

Goldman Sachs' European weekly report pointed out that the Stoxx 600 index fell 1.1% last week, but the market was not generally down. Driven by rising oil prices, GSCI Energy was the best-performing asset class with a weekly return of +6.6%. Momentum stocks, on the other hand, have retraced significantly.

Goldman Sachs believes that this is more a technical adjustment and position rotation rather than a deterioration in fundamentals. The report emphasizes that the earnings revision trend of European companies is still positive, and EPS growth expectations are still improving, which provides solid support for the market.

This shows that the market’s dominant logic is shifting from simply “chasing rising momentum” to “fundamental verification.” One-sentence conclusion: The momentum retracement in the European market is a healthy adjustment.

The rise of the energy sector and solid earnings fundamentals indicate that the driving force behind the market's subsequent rise will be more balanced and sustainable. Positive/negative: Structurally positive for the energy sector. Momentum stocks that have experienced excessive early gains are short-term negative.

The market has responded to this rotation, but the momentum switch may not be complete yet. Catalysts:

1) Core data of the European earnings season, verifying fundamentals;

2) Whether oil prices can continue to run at high levels;

3) European Central Bank interest rate decision and speech.

Read more: https://t.co/KVBHjQUyKq

Street Signal | India Nifty trades sideways, domestic inflows improve, 2Q profits expected to grow healthily (Goldman Sachs)

Goldman Sachs India weekly report showed that the Nifty index was basically flat last week despite fluctuations in oil prices. In terms of capital, foreign capital continued to flow in, and domestic stock market capital inflows improved by 27% month-on-month in June, reaching US$3.1 billion per month.

This is a core factor underpinning India's market valuations. Looking forward, market consensus expects 2Q earnings to achieve healthy growth, with the performance of the IT services and financial sectors being particularly critical.

The current high valuation of the Indian market relies on the realization of earnings growth, so the upcoming earnings season is an important test of the legitimacy of its valuation. One-sentence conclusion: The Indian market has shown resilience under the impact of oil prices, and its abundant domestic liquidity is its solid backing.

However, the next direction of the market will depend on whether the upcoming earnings data can support the current high valuation. Positives/negatives: Structural benefits to the Indian market, especially the financial, IT and industrial sectors. Short-term volatility may increase, depending on earnings results.

Domestic institutional funds continue to flow in, and foreign capital inflows have improved, but these factors may have been reflected in valuations. Catalysts:

1) Core heavyweights such as HCL Tech, Reliance and HDFC Bank will release 1Q financial reports in the next two weeks;

2) The impact of monsoon rainfall on agriculture and the economy;

3) The persistence of foreign capital inflows.

Read more: https://t.co/6Od7a9NeOi

Street Signal | U.S. bond yields are linked under the impact of oil prices, and "energy speed bumps" affect the path of global interest rates (Goldman Sachs)

Goldman Sachs' rate trader report revealed an important development in the market last week: U.S. Treasury yields are highly correlated with oil prices. Data show that when Brent crude oil rose from $71/barrel to $79/barrel, the 2-year and 10-year U.S. Treasury yields surged by 12 and 16 basis points respectively.

The transmission mechanism behind this is that rising oil prices have pushed up inflation expectations, which in turn strengthened market expectations that the Federal Reserve will maintain tightening policies, leading to a sell-off in bonds.

The report defines this phenomenon as an "energy speed bump" and incorporates it into the global interest rate forecast model, believing that this will have a spillover effect on other markets, including Canada and the euro zone. One-sentence conclusion: Oil prices have become the main driving force for short-term interest rate fluctuations.

The transmission chain of "energy-inflation-interest rates" is becoming increasingly tight. Investors need to pay close attention to this macro risk factor. Positive/negative: negative for the global bond market, especially long-term bonds. Positive for TIPS and energy stocks.

The market has responded to rising oil prices, but if oil prices remain high, the secondary impact on interest rates has not yet been fully priced in. Catalysts:

1) The next direction of Brent crude oil prices;

2) U.S. CPI data this week, verifying the transmission of oil prices to core inflation;

3) Fed officials’ stance on the impact of oil prices.

Read more: https://t.co/yc8QS9yntl

Street Signal | COSCO Shipping Energy's 2Q26 profit is in line with expectations, and it will accelerate its pursuit from 3Q after the trapped oil tanker is released (Goldman Sachs)

A Goldman Sachs report pointed out that COSCO Shipping Energy (https://t.co/VOXTn22e1C)’s 2Q26 net profit was approximately 2.3 billion yuan (a 7% month-on-month increase), which was basically in line with expectations.

However, due to the blockade of the Persian Gulf, seven oil tankers were trapped and demurrage was unable to be collected, and its revenue contribution was lower than that of its peers. With the Strait of Hormuz reopened and the trapped oil tanker released, the company expects to catch up with its peers from 3Q26.

Goldman Sachs is optimistic that interest rates will return to an annual average of $150,000 per day and maintains a positive rating. The core logic is that the limited supply of tankers (few new ship orders) and the improvement in demand structure (long-distance transportation brought about by geopolitics) jointly support the upward trend of the economy.

The market's previous concerns were mainly focused on the short-term risk of the ship being stuck, while ignoring the huge resilience after release. One sentence conclusion: COSCO Shipping Energy's "disturbance" is temporary. As the trapped ships resume operations and the peak season approaches, its profit elasticity will be released.

It is a target with clear rebound momentum in the shipping sector. Positive/negative: Positive for COSCO Shipping Energy (https://t.co/VOXTn22e1C). The weaker-than-expected 2Q has been partially reflected in the stock price, but market expectations for catching up in 3Q have not yet been fully established, and there is room for upside. Catalysts:

1) The actual revenue contribution of 7 released tankers in 3Q;

2) The trend of crude oil transportation freight rates (TD3C, etc.);

3) The long-term stability of the situation in the Strait of Hormuz.

Read more: https://t.co/T20APNspbH

《2026年北美国别市场观察报告》

2025年美加墨（USMCA）三国贸易总额约1.59万亿美元，贸易节奏深度协同。墨西哥已取代加拿大及中国，成为美国第一大贸易伙伴。美国能源供给与制造业高度依赖邻国，加拿大提供美炼油厂23%的原油摄入量，墨西哥则承接了美国近一半的机动车零配件出口。宏观层面，美国通胀2.7%、失业率4.4%，加拿大通胀2.2%、失业率6.8%。AI基础设施投资驱动明显，罗素3000相关企业营收预期较2023年3月累计增加3000亿美元，AI投资占GDP峰值预期为2%。约49%的美国公司已将IT与软件开发近岸外包至加墨地区，北美近岸远程人才规模快速增长。

政策架构呈现“美国优先制度化”与“加拿大对冲性补贴”的双轨特征。美国通过《芯片与科学法案》（专项527亿美元）、《通胀削减法案》及《大而美法案》构建半导体与清洁能源补贴屏障，且CFIUS审查已扩围至AI、量子及能源等敏感领域，并针对特定对手（含中国）实施严厉准入限制。加拿大则通过战略响应基金（SRF）、战略创新基金（SIF）及加拿大增长基金（CGF）应对关税冲击，提供高比例成本报销，以撬动低碳技术与先进制造投入。美墨定于2026年5月启动USMCA首轮自动审议谈判，关键争议涉及原产地规则及关键矿产供应链安全。

产业布局上，中国企业倾向采用“中国研发部件+墨西哥组装+北美销售”的三角模式，选址向墨西哥北部蒙特雷集聚。信息技术市场预计年复合增长率（CAGR）为7.81%，智能制造市场规模预计2030年达857.2亿美元。金融科技领域预计CAGR为13.9%。中国企业在墨产能布局（如海信产能1000万台）虽享受零关税，但受EAR管制及337调查风险影响。未来USMCA审议可能收紧原产地规则，将直接威胁现有供应链合规性，企业需在谈判前完成原产地链路审计与合规风险对冲。

【关键词】：#USMCA #近岸外包 #原产地规则 #CFIUS #芯片与科学法案 #IRA #大而美法案 #SRF #SIF #CGF #美墨加谈判 #AI投资 #劳动生产率 #罗素3000 #蒙特雷 #海辰储能 #海信墨西哥 #美的北美 #智能制造 #布伦特油价 #EIA预测 #金融科技 #CETA #CPTPP #供应链审计 #合规风险 #EAR管制 #337调查 #近岸外包成本 #北美出海

【观点】：

报告属于典型的信息汇编型研究，数据源涵盖WTO、美国普查局、高盛及德勤等，可信度中等偏上。然而，必须区分“事实引用”与“投行假设”。高盛关于“生成式AI十年内提升美国劳动生产率15%且GDP增量4.5万亿美元”的预测属于情景假设，并非已实现事实，若投资者直接将其作为决策依据，面临极高的模型误差风险。

三个核心变量决定了北美出海的成败：一是USMCA 2026年审议的潜在不确定性，原产地规则收紧将直接击穿中国企业在墨组装的“免税合规”逻辑，该风险是当前最大的单点失效点，报告虽提及但权重过低。二是AI资本支出（Capex）的传导节奏，高盛预判的“峰值—回落”曲线意味着算力基建需求可能面临降速，加墨地区作为外包承接地，将经历从算力配套向软件应用侧的转型，企业需提前布局业务迁移路径。三是美欧关税僵局的外溢效应，美国对欧汽车加征25%关税若落地，势必导致欧洲车企竞相在北美（尤其是墨西哥）构建产能，从而挤占中国企业现有的集群空间与资源，这是报告未充分探讨的二阶影响。

逻辑盲点显著：报告将“近岸外包成本比美国低80%”泛化至加墨市场，这存在严重的统计口径误导。80%的成本优势仅适用于中美洲或加勒比海更低端的离岸人力，墨西哥软开成本实际仅比美国低约35%-50%。此外，报告未提示CFIUS对“穿透审查”的风险，即当中国背景企业在墨工厂的中方持股比例超过美方下游客户的合规阈值时，即便产品合规，依然会因下游客户的主动避险而被剔除出供应链。

抽象层级需提升至“供应链地缘政治”高度：北美出海已从简单的“制造外迁”演变为“合规身份管理”。企业不再仅仅是卖产品，而是在美墨边境通过资本合规与资产所有权穿透，管理一种“身份资产”。决策含义在于：企业必须将“地缘政治条款”写入商业合同，特别是针对原产地规则突变的成本分摊与解约机制。对能源侧而言，EIA预测的布伦特原油均价下行周期，隐含全球油市过剩假设，若OPEC+政策博弈超预期，原油价格波动将直接反噬墨西哥清洁能源项目的投资回报率。企业需在战略规划中引入敏感性测试，而非直接采纳投行基准预测。
matchedQuery: 高盛 原油 报告

🚨 【别怪我没提醒！下周全球市场要“变天”，你的钱袋子即将迎来三连击！】

text:
#GIFTNIFTY is down by -34 points. 👎

As of the latest close (Friday, July 10/11, 2026), WTI crude oil is at approximately $71.50 per barrel.

Will the price go above $76–$77 per barrel? 🤔
#CrudeOil prices can fluctuate with weekend news on geopolitics (e.g., US-Iran tensions) or supply data.

US-Iran ceasefire got disintegrated and caused the disruption in the highly critical Strait of #Hormuz shipping lane. This would surely put pressure on oil-import dependent markets like India.

Missiles are flying in the Middle East, a critical global energy chokepoint was just declared "closed," and Bitcoin is chilling right around $64,000 like it’s a regular Sunday.

While the mainstream media screams panic over the US launching a third round of airstrikes in Iran - prompting Tehran to strike back at Jordan and Qatar and declare the Strait of Hormuz closed - crypto is flat-out refusing to dump.

The smart money isn’t buying the geopolitical doomsday narrative. Here is the exact institutional breakdown:

• The Energy Bluff: Unlike the massive oil spike back in March, WTI crude is sitting flat right around $71. Real-time vessel tracking reveals that maritime traffic is quietly ignoring the "shutdown" and sailing right through the Strait anyway. The market smells a bluff.

• The Institutional Shield: The brutal two-month capital hemorrhage has officially snapped. US Spot Bitcoin and Ethereum ETFs just broke an 8-week, $8 billion losing streak, pulling in +$281.8M in combined net inflows this week.

• The Hidden Catch: Don’t pop the champagne just yet. If you look closely at the underlying tape, this isn't a broad market recovery. This entire reversal is being driven almost exclusively by BlackRock’s $IBIT, while rival funds sit completely stagnant. A single institutional titan is carrying the floor on its back.

The Bottom Line: Crypto is decoupling from geopolitical theater because structural institutional buyers are aggressively absorbing the risk. The true test of this resilience arrives the second Wall Street opens its gates on Monday morning. Watch the oil tape closely. 🛢️🐋

Nearly 20% of every litre of petrol sold in India today is domestically produced ethanol. That ethanol is procured at around ₹71 per litre, a price that does not fluctuate every morning with Brent crude, geopolitical conflicts or shipping disruptions. https://t.co/lQ7UIn0W9D

⚓ HORMUZ BLOCKADE: Should Investors Panic? 📉

​Iran moving to close the Strait of Hormuz has sent global macro triggers into overdrive. With 20% of global oil passing through this narrow chokepoint, Dalal Street is bracing for impact.

​Here is your quick Techno-Funda Playbook to navigate the volatility:

​💥 The Macro Threat:
A sustained surge in Brent crude pinches India's import bills, pressures the INR, and brings imported inflation risk back to the table.

​🔴 Sectors Facing Margin Heat:
​Paints & Chemicals: Crude derivative costs spike.

​OMCs: Bearing the brunt of raw input inflation.

​Aviation & Tyres: Direct hit from rising fuel/synthetic rubber costs.

​🟢 The Defensive Safe Havens:
​Pharma & IT: Insulated business models + IT gets a mild tailwind from a weaker Rupee.

​Banking: Structural credit growth acts as the ultimate index anchor.

​📊 Nifty Levels to Watch:
​Support: Critical structural floor at 23,800 – 24,000. A daily close below 23,800 opens the gates for deeper corrections.

​Resistance: Big supply pressure at 24,400 – 24,600.

​💡 The Strategy: Geopolitical shocks trigger sharp, algorithmic corrections, not permanent structural collapses. 

Avoid panic-selling; watch Brent crude stability and FII fund flows instead.

​#StockMarketIndia #Nifty50 #StraitofHormuz #Investing #MacroEconomy

Crude Oil Prices Drop Despite US-Iran Tensions as Nigerian Fuel Prices Continue to Fall

Brent crude and West Texas Intermediate (WTI) declined to $76 and $71 per barrel respectively on Saturday, despite renewed airstrikes between the United States and Iran.

US President Donald Trump had earlier announced the end of the ceasefire with Iran, and by extension Israel, triggering a brief surge in global crude oil prices. However, the upward momentum cooled off by Thursday and Saturday.
The renewed hostilities in the Middle East initially sparked speculation about possible increases in domestic fuel prices in Nigeria. Fortunately, the effect was short-lived as crude oil prices eased successively.

Relief for Nigerian Consumers
According to reports, Dangote Refinery’s gantry price and depot owners’ ex-depot prices have dropped to ₦1,075 per litre following at least four successive reductions linked to falling crude oil prices.
This reduction has been passed down to consumers, with retail petrol pump prices falling by between ₦131 and ₦167 per litre. As of Sunday, July 12, 2026, petrol is now sold at between ₦1,150 and ₦1,205 per litre in Abuja and environs — down from ₦1,317 to ₦1,336 per litre on June 18, 2026.

Outlook Remains Positive
In an interview, the National President of the Petroleum Products Retail Outlets Owners Association of Nigeria, Billy Gillis-Harry, expressed optimism that the downward trend will continue.

“The fuel price will continue to drop. We have seen this in the past three weeks with the Dangote refinery and petrol retailers and marketers amid falling crude oil prices. One thing constant with the downstream sector is price volatility.”

Nigerian motorists and households are currently enjoying a welcome relief at the pumps as global oil market dynamics and local refining capacity combine to drive prices lower.

WTI Crude Oil / GOLD — Monthly

The green line is the S&P 500. Monthly RSI is already at a historical low, which is consistent with oil rising and gold easing from here. With RSI not yet at 70 and price still early in a longer-term uptrend, oil has room to run. Notably, when this ratio enters a sustained uptrend, the S&P has tended to do the same. Most importantly, if this ratio turns into a longer-term downtrend before 2031, that would be consistent with Scenario 1 for BTC.

My base case is this chart will hit the red rectangle zone which works as the resistance line for a decade. After hitting this zone, we can expect long-term bear trend in this chart.

TL;DR: Parsi’s chart uses a real SPR decline but strips away the context to create panic. The SPR still holds about 319.5 million barrels, and the U.S. also has roughly 411.4 million barrels in commercial inventories, for about 730.8 million barrels combined.

Much of the SPR release was structured as a loan, not a permanent loss. Companies are expected to return an average of 1.28 barrels for every barrel borrowed, which could rebuild the reserve above 400 million barrels through 2028.

A cropped SPR chart does not prove action against the Islamic Republic is unwise. It promotes fear and paralysis, which benefits the regime.

=============

@tparsi is using a real decline in the Strategic Petroleum Reserve to create a misleading sense of panic and suggest that the United States is “running out of options” against the Islamic Republic.

That conclusion is not supported by his graph.

His chart begins near a recent high, ends immediately after a deliberate emergency release, and truncates the y-axis at roughly 315 million barrels instead of showing zero or the full historical record. This visually exaggerates the decline and encourages readers to interpret a low reserve level as national helplessness.

Yes, the SPR fell to 319.5 million barrels by July 3, its lowest level since 1983. But the United States also held 411.4 million barrels in commercial crude inventories, bringing total crude stocks to 730.8 million barrels. The SPR is important, but it is not the entirety of America’s oil supply or energy capability. 

He also omits why the reserve declined. This was not an unexplained disappearance of oil. All 32 International Energy Agency members unanimously agreed to make 400 million barrels available after severe supply disruptions in the Middle East and the Strait of Hormuz. The United States committed 172 million barrels as part of that coordinated international response. 

Most importantly, much of the American release was structured as an exchange, not a permanent loss. Companies borrowing oil must return the barrels plus a premium. The United States has contracted to loan about 133 million barrels so far and expects to receive an average of 1.28 barrels back for every barrel released. Those returns are expected to begin later in 2026 and continue through 2028, potentially raising the SPR above 400 million barrels. 

Reuters also reports that reserve replenishment around the world could add approximately 506,000 barrels per day of crude demand in late 2026 and as much as 664,000 barrels per day by the third quarter of 2027. In other words, governments are already planning how to rebuild these reserves. 

This graph does not prove that military action against the Islamic Republic is unwise. A lower SPR is a strategic cost that must be managed, not a veto on action.

Whether action is wise depends on its objectives, probability of success, escalation risks, the regime’s nuclear program, threats to shipping, its proxy network, attacks on American interests, and the long-term cost of allowing the Islamic Republic to continue expanding its capabilities.

Parsi’s selectively framed oil chart answers none of those questions.

Whether intentional or not, presenting this as proof that America is nearly out of options creates panic, encourages paralysis, and serves the Islamic Republic’s interests.

Crude Oil Weekly Outlook - July 6-10, 2026

Top News Story: WTI Rebounds 4% as Ceasefire Strains and Trump Post Reignite Hormuz Volatility

Crude oil futures snapped their four-week losing streak in dramatic fashion, with NYMEX WTI rebounding to settle at $71.27 on Friday. The calm that characterized late June shattered mid-week after a severe flare-up in regional attacks on July 7-8 threatened the stability of the temporary U.S.-Iran diplomatic framework. 

Speculative short-covering accelerated rapidly on Wednesday when WTI rocketed over $4 to an intraday high of $76.08 following a social media post from President Trump declaring that "the Cease Fire is OVER!" Overwhelming prompt-delivery panic returned to the tape as observed tanker transit through the Strait of Hormuz plummeted again. 

However, the vertical spike lost its legs into the weekend, retracing sharply on Friday after both Washington and Tehran confirmed that despite the renewed hostilities, back-channel technical discussions in Oman and a Qatari diplomatic delegation in Iran are actively working to preserve the broader 60-day memorandum of understanding.

Market Notes

The physical cash market immediately absorbed the geopolitical whiplash, sending the Plains WTI Posting higher to finish the tracking period at $67.89 on Friday. In the retail sector, the multi-week drop in fuel costs hit an abrupt wall; AAA reported that the national average for regular gasoline reversed its downward trajectory overnight, climbing to $3.84/gal as wholesale gasoline components rallied. Industrial demand in the West remains robust, but the International Energy Agency (IEA) issued a stern warning on Friday stating that this renewed friction risks entirely derailing critical efforts to rebuild depleted global oil inventories later this year. 

In a notable shift in regional strategy, the United Arab Emirates (UAE) countered the regional blockade by accelerating crude oil production to an all-time high last month, presenting a stark operational contrast to its Persian Gulf neighbors who remain hampered by the naval bottlenecks.

Price Summary and Recent Movements

A "whiplash reversal" week as WTI successfully defended its cyclical lows near $67.00 before surging to test key overhead moving averages. Support has shifted back to the $68.00–$68.50 corridor, while $76.50 now represents the primary technical ceiling for the bulls.

NYMEX WTI Front-Month (August 2026) Price Action:

Friday Settle: $71.27

Daily Change: -$0.81 (-1.12% drop on Friday as weekend peace talks resumed)

Weekly Change: +$2.80 (Up 4.1% on the week, snapping the month-long slide)
Technical View: Reclaiming the $71 baseline keeps the broader medium-term chart in a volatile, range-bound posture. The Relative Strength Index (RSI) bounced off oversold lows to settle at a neutral 48.5, indicating a balanced paper market ahead of next week's sessions.

Forward Curve (Line-by-Line):
September 2026: $71.20 (August/September prompt spread holding tightly at a thin +$0.07 backwardation)

October 2026: $71.01 (September/October spread widening slightly to +$0.19)

November 2026: $70.75

December 2026: $70.40

Key Market Factors
Hormuz Ceasefire Strain: Renewed localized attacks cut weekly shipping transits through the chokepoint significantly, reminding macro traders that the physical return of Middle Eastern supply remains completely vulnerable to political crossfire.

Bearish IEA Demand Print: Capping the upside, the IEA's Friday report projected that global oil demand will contract by 1 million bpd in 2026—the first decline since 2020—driven heavily by a massive 9% demand loss in China as high prices and EV adoption accelerate structural fuel savings.

Surprise EIA Inventory Build: The EIA reported a 3-million-barrel build in domestic commercial crude inventories to 411.4 million barrels. While a build is fundamentally bearish, the fact that overall U.S. stocks remain 6% below the five-year average limited the downside.

UAE Production Surge: The UAE's bold move to push output to record highs provided a critical alternative supply buffer for global refiners, blunting what could have been a much larger price spike on Trump’s social media declarations.

Upcoming Report Expectations
The entirety of the oil complex will spend the weekend monitoring tracking data in the Persian Gulf and diplomatic leaks out of Muscat and Tehran to see if the technical teams successfully salvage the truce. Moving into next week, traders will focus on the July 15 EIA Petroleum Status Report to evaluate whether the recent spike back into the 70s slows down domestic refinery utilization rates, which are currently running hard to capture strong seasonal cracks.

Funds Activity

The Commitment of Traders (COT) analytics captured a violent "bear trap" unfolding mid-week. Systematic trend-following systems and managed money accounts that were heavily shorting the breakdown below $69 were forced into aggressive long liquidation and short-covering when the July 8 headline spike struck the board. 

Conversely, independent commercial producers used the brief run toward $76 to lock in aggressive hedging floors for late 2026 production. At the same time, retail and industrial end-users stepped in on Friday’s pullback to accumulate prompt protection against further summer baseline escalation.

Outlook for the Week Ahead

The outlook is neutral-to-volatile as the market balances a clear macro demand slowdown in Asia against the persistent threat of immediate, physical supply disruptions in the Middle East.

Strategy: The return of the $71 handle demonstrates that the geopolitical risk premium cannot be completely counted out. For commercial operations and agricultural entities looking to manage late-summer and harvest-season fuel needs, chasing this mid-week rally was a losing proposition. 

Maintain a patient, programmatic approach: look to scale into physical diesel and fuel supplies on any localized, headline-driven pullbacks toward the $68.50 support floor, while leaving targets open to lock in extended 4Q26 coverage if diplomacy prevails and tests the $65 structural baseline.

#CrudeOil #WTI #OilPrices

*CPI Tuesday 
*PPI Wednesday
*Retail Sales Thursday
*Housing/industrial-production data and consumer sentiment Friday

-Tuesday (July 14): NFIB Small Business Optimism Index, Consumer Price Index (CPI), Core CPI, Net Long-Term TIC Flows

-Thursday (July 16): Retail Sales, Initial Jobless Claims, Continuing Claims, Philadelphia Fed Manufacturing Index, Business Inventories, Pending Home Sales, EIA Natural Gas Inventories
-Friday (July 17): Import Prices, Export Prices, Housing Starts, Building Permits, Industrial Production, Capacity Utilization, NAHB Housing Market Index, University of Michigan Consumer Sentiment — Preliminary

HOW TO AVERT THE IMPENDING OIL CRISIS

U.S. Oil Producers' Reluctance to Expand Output: Market Uncertainty and a Path Forward Through Strategic Guarantees

U.S. oil companies remain cautious about significantly increasing production, primarily due to uncertainty regarding future market absorption. Recent forecasts from the International Energy Agency (IEA) project a potential global oil surplus of up to approximately 5 million barrels per day in 2027, assuming recovery of flows through the Strait of Hormuz.

In such an environment, committing capital to new drilling would carry substantial risk. However, with global oil inventories currently at depleted levels following recent supply disruptions, any emerging surplus could be absorbed over an extended period—likely more than a year—through restocking by governments and commercial entities, thereby mitigating the prospect of an immediate glut.

Geopolitical instability in the Strait of Hormuz introduces further complexity. Should tensions persist, the anticipated surplus may fail to materialize; conversely, prolonged disruptions could exacerbate the current tight supply conditions. Oil producers, bound by fiduciary duties to shareholders, require greater predictability before scaling operations. The industry has previously incurred significant losses from over-drilling in response to temporary demand signals, reinforcing a disciplined approach to capital deployment.

In the event of a sustained oil crisis, governments worldwide would need to intervene to safeguard economic stability anyways. Absent clearer demand visibility in this volatile environment, the outlook for rapid production increases in non-GCC countries remains challenging.

A Viable Solution: Government-Backed Oil Purchase Guarantees 

One mechanism that could provide the necessary confidence for producers to accelerate new drilling is a guaranteed buyer at a predetermined price. If the U.S. government—and potentially other nations with untapped reserves—were to fund or support exploration and drilling of new wells while committing to purchase all incremental production for Strategic Petroleum Reserve (SPR) replenishment and global stock builds, companies would view this as a low-risk, assured investment. Such arrangements could offer returns comparable in stability to government treasuries, enabling producers to strengthen balance sheets and attract further capital.

This arrangement won't be easy, The main challenge aside from political backing and how to price this future oil output would be how to structure and price in the associated industrial risk factors. However, that's a complex topic for seasoned industry experts and policy makers, so I'll leave that to them.

This approach represents a potential win-win. Governments already plan to refill strategic reserves; redirecting a portion of those appropriations toward new domestic and allied production could meaningfully boost global supply while enhancing energy security. An even more robust framework would involve a multilateral agreement among a small group of nations to collectively purchase excess output until new wells reach the end of their productive lives. Beyond de-risking investments, such collaboration could generate positive public relations for the industry by demonstrating proactive contributions to averting global economic instability from future oil crises.

Implementing this through structured, transparent contracts—potentially modeled on established take-or-pay precedents in the energy sector—would balance taxpayer protections with production incentives. With proper safeguards, including price mechanisms and reasonable environmental standards, this strategy could help bridge short-term uncertainties and support long-term energy market resilience.

In this context, shale wells are particularly well-suited to such a program. Unlike conventional fields that can produce steadily for decades, modern oil fracking wells deliver rapid initial ramp-up—often reaching peak output within months—followed by steep early declines that front-load the majority of their production into the first few years. This profile enables a swift boost to global supply for strategic stockpiling while naturally limiting long-term purchase obligations as output tapers. Governments could thus achieve meaningful near-term energy security gains with more contained fiscal exposure, providing producers the certainty they need to drill without creating open-ended liabilities. By aligning government offtake guarantees with the inherent production dynamics of shale, this approach offers an agile, pragmatic bridge toward greater market stability.

Furthermore, the United States and allied nations should actively pursue strategic partnerships with Argentina to accelerate development of its world-class Vaca Muerta shale formation. This play ranks among the largest and most prospective unconventional resources globally, with high-quality reservoirs that support rapid production growth and competitive breakeven costs. By extending guaranteed purchase agreements and technical/financial collaboration, the U.S. could help Argentina overcome infrastructure bottlenecks, attract additional investment, and scale output significantly. Such cooperation would not only diversify global supply and enhance collective energy security in line with the Donroe Doctrine, but also deliver substantial economic benefits to Argentina—including job creation, export revenues, and broader GDP growth—while strengthening bilateral ties with one of America's greatest allies in the Western Hemisphere. Argentina’s recent policy momentum and openness to international collaboration make it an ideal candidate for this model, creating a mutually beneficial pathway to expand non-OPEC supply.
